policy_statement: Expand the E-Park program in the Center City to reduce driving around to find parking.
mechanism_description: The policy expands an E-Park program to cut down on driving around to find parking, reducing vehicle travel and associated transport emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Transport activity (↓ cruising/VMT) → ↓ fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes the transport/parking system by providing smarter parking availability/management so drivers spend less time and distance searching for parking. (2) This reduces engine runtime and distance traveled (less idling and circling), cutting gasoline/diesel combustion. (3) The outcome is lower transport-sector GHG emissions (primarily CO₂; also potential reductions in black carbon and other pollutants).
dominant_pathway_test: By reducing parking-search cruising, the policy’s single biggest climate-relevant effect is lowering VMT and fuel combustion, thereby reducing transport GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: smart parking reduces cruising → reduces VMT/fuel use → reduces GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: the principal effect is direct emissions reduction rather than improving resource productivity as the primary aim (Resource Efficiency) or reducing climate hazard exposure (Adaptation) or enhancing ecosystems (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: if implemented mainly to reduce congestion/air pollution or improve user convenience, the climate-relevant pathway still runs through reduced fuel combustion; if dynamic pricing shifts mode choice or induces demand, net effects depend on design but the intended dominant pathway remains emissions reduction from reduced cruising.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any “smart parking management to reduce parking search” mechanism operates via reduced vehicle travel/engine time and thus is fundamentally a mitigation mechanism.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., demand management vs ITS optimization) is deferred to policy-level evidence and implementation details.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential rebound/offsets if easier parking increases driving or if pricing changes shift demand; net GHG impact depends on pricing/enforcement and broader transport context, but the canonical mechanism’s dominant pathway is still emissions reduction via reduced cruising.
key_indicators: “Expand the E-Park program”; “reduce the need to drive around to find parking”
co_benefits: Reduced congestion, improved local air quality, reduced noise, time savings for drivers
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be implemented via dynamic pricing (pricing-mechanism) or operational policy changes, but the text emphasizes program expansion (deployment/scale-up) without specifying pricing or regulatory elements, so infrastructure-investment is the best fit.
